description.task: Demonstrate compile-time polymorphism by creating an overloaded printInfo() method that accepts different parameter types (String, int, double).
instructions: Define three static methods all named printInfo.
Each should print: 'String value: Hello', 'Integer value: 42', 'Double value: 3.14'.
Java distinguishes them by the parameter type (compile-time resolution).
description.hint: Compile-time polymorphism (method overloading) is resolved by the compiler based on the number and type of arguments at the call site.
description.expectedOutput:
String value: Hello
Integer value: 42
Double value: 3.14

description.task: Create a Shape base class and two subclasses (Circle, Rectangle). Use a Shape reference to call the overridden area() method on each object.
description.context: Runtime polymorphism (dynamic dispatch) means the JVM decides which overridden method to call at runtime based on the actual object type, not the reference type.
instructions: Implement Circle with constructor Circle(double radius) and override area().
Implement Rectangle with constructor Rectangle(double width, double height) and override area().
The key demonstration: s1 and s2 are of type Shape, but the overridden method of the actual object runs.
description.hint: Math.PI * 7.0 * 7.0 ≈ 153.94. Use @Override annotation on each area() method.

description.task: Store multiple Shape objects in a Shape array, then iterate and call area() on each. Java will dispatch the correct overridden method at runtime.
description.context: Storing different subtype objects in a parent-type array and calling their overridden methods is a core real-world use of runtime polymorphism.
instructions: Define Shape, Circle, and Rectangle classes as in the previous task (can be in the same file).
The for-each loop calls s.area() — the JVM picks Circle.area() or Rectangle.area() based on actual object type.
description.expectedOutput:
Area: 28.27
Area: 16.00
Area: 78.54
Area: 36.00
Total area: 158.81
